old_claim.note: Philosophy is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains from philosophy to codified_law. Early Mesopotamian written law-code evidence does not require classical philosophy as an input.

source_shape.source_locator: The Ur-Nammu Law Code, Description and Commentary: dates and describes a Sumerian cuneiform law-code witness centuries before the classical philosophy node.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports early written Mesopotamian law-code artifacts without invoking philosophy as a prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Philosophy may influence later jurisprudence, but it is not source-backed as a direct prerequisite for this ancient written-code scope.

ontology_before: The graph modeled philosophy as context for codified_law.
ontology_after: The graph separates written law-code artifacts from later legal philosophy or jurisprudence.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: philosophy is absent as a direct prerequisite for codified_law.
would_reject_if: A source showed the scoped ancient written law codes depended on a philosophical tradition.
The node were explicitly rescoped to jurisprudential codification rather than written law-code artifacts.
short_reason: Classical philosophy is not a direct prerequisite for Mesopotamian written law codes.
